data.title: Lindsay Corporation: It's Too Soon For An Upgrade
data.description:
Over the last several months now, Lindsay Corporation (LNN) has had a bit of a rough time. Even though the company ended 2025 with higher revenue, profits, and cash flows than it had in 2024, a big decline in backlog, combined with concerns about the economy more broadly, proved problematic. The picture was made worse when, for the first quarter of 2026, management announced a decline in all of these metrics year over year. As a result of this, the stock has actually fallen 12.5% since I last reaffirmed it as a Hold candidate back in September of last year. For context, the S&P 500 (SP500) is down just 1.4%.

Even though I fully expect market conditions to worsen before they get better, I do think that the company is getting closer to being attractive enough to justify a Buy rating. But I'm not ready to make that jump just yet. Based on how the stock is valued, combined with expectations of further weakening moving forward, remaining cautious is the right choice.

Of course, my assessment of the matter could change as new data comes in. But right now, expectations point toward continued weakness. You see, before the market opens on April 2nd, the management team at the company is expected to announce financial results for the second quarter of the firm's 2026 fiscal year. As we approach that date, the thought is that revenue and profitability will decline. So unless we see something big and unexpected occur that happens to be a positive for the company, I believe that maintaining it as a Hold candidate is the most appropriate course of action.

Checking in on Lindsay Corporation

Perhaps the best place to start when it comes to analyzing Lindsay Corporation right now would be to talk about exactly what analysts expect from the upcoming earnings release. As I mentioned already, before the market opens on April 2nd, the firm is going to be announcing financial performance for the second quarter of its 2026 fiscal year. The thought right now from analysts is that revenue will come in at $171.4 million. Although that's a nice chunk of change, it would actually represent a decline of 8.4% compared to the $187.1 million that the company reported a year earlier.

This drop in revenue is expected to be accompanied by weakening profits and cash flows also. Take earnings per share as an example. Analysts currently believe that it will generate $1.69 per share in profits. That is a big drop from the $2.44 reported at the same time last year, and it would mean a drop in net income from $26.6 million to $18.1 million. Analysts have not provided estimates when it comes to cash flows. But in the table above, you can see what cash flows were for the second quarter of 2025. In all likelihood, declining revenue and profits will translate to weaker cash flows as well.

At first glance, this might seem peculiar. After all, as the chart above illustrates, revenue actually jumped from 2024 to 2025. As it expanded, profitability followed suit. Net income grew from $66.3 million to $74.1 million. Even more impressive was operating cash flow, which shot up from $95.8 million to $132.9 million. If we strip out changes in working capital, we do get a smaller but still impressive increase from $90.1 million to $99.9 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business grew from $97.4 million to $108.6 million.

The weakness for the company really began to show up in revenue and profitability in the first quarter of 2026. The chart above shows a decline in every metric that I looked at for that time. Given the nature of the business, it's not unexpected to see some volatility. I could point to multiple examples here. But perhaps the most interesting one involves its Road Zipper technology. For the uninitiated, this is a barrier system that management developed whereby T-shaped concrete and steel barriers are connected in order to form a continuous wall. The firm also has a barrier transfer machine as part of this system that can move the barrier laterally across the pavement.

The goal here is to be able to quickly and easily erect and move barriers that are necessary for things like road work. But by management's own admission, revenue associated with this part of the company is incredibly volatile and often hinges on a small number of sizable contracts that come and go.

I would be lying to you if I said that was the only reason why the firm has seen some volatility. To understand exactly what is going on here, it would be helpful to dig deeper. In the chart below, you can see financial performance for the last three fiscal years, as well as for the first quarter of 2026 compared to the same time in 2025, associated with the company’s Infrastructure segment. This is the same part of the firm that includes its Road Zipper System. But it also consists of other offerings like crash cushions, specialty barriers, road marking and safety equipment, rail products, and the like.

This is actually the best part of the company right now, given its overall financial trajectory in recent years. Even during the first quarter of 2026, revenue and profits were higher than they were a year earlier. But a lot of what this segment does relies on state and federal government funding. For instance, management pointed out the $110 billion of incremental federal funding for things like roads, bridges, and other transportation projects under the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act. That funding is expected to run through September of this year. Unfortunately, there have been some issues, such as inflation associated with material prices and labor costs, that have offset this. But with only 88% of the funds having been committed to States and only 58% of them having been reimbursed to the states, there is still plenty of capital to go around.

By comparison, the Irrigation equipment has become more of an issue. After seeing revenue and profits increase from 2024 to 2025, we saw a decline in both in the first quarter of 2026 compared to the same time the previous year.

According to management, both North America and international markets experienced declining irrigation revenue on a year-over-year basis, with the former down 4% while the latter was down 15%. In North America, the drop was because of a reduction in unit sales volume that more than offset higher average selling prices. And when it comes to the international market, the company saw weakness because of Brazil and Western Europe, on top of lower project revenues in the MENA (Middle East and North Africa) region.

It appears as though high-interest rates and credit challenges have proven to be problematic in Brazil specifically. But I do think that here at home is more relevant. After all, North America was responsible for 55.7% of segment revenue during that time.

A big challenge for a company such as Lindsay Corporation, at least so far as it comes to the Irrigation segment, is the fact that net farm income has been in an interesting spot. According to the USDA, it did total $154.5 billion in 2024. That was up from the $127.5 billion reported a year earlier. But there are some caveats here that need to be explained.

A big reason for this surge stemmed from the fact that, year over year, federal government funding jumped $20.4 billion. Without this, net farm income would have risen a more modest 5.2% year over year. But when we start looking at a breakdown of this data, it does become disconcerting. Net farm income includes cash receipts from not only crops but also animals and products as well. Year over year, crop-related cash receipts dropped from $244.9 billion to $238.1 billion. This marked the third consecutive year of declines in this regard. And in fact, you would have to go back to 2020 to see crop cash receipts come in lower than what they were last year. There's also the expectation that we won't really see actual recovery this year, with it inching up only slightly to $240.8 billion.

According to the data provided by the USDA, on an inflation-adjusted basis, total cash receipts for the industry should fall by $24.3 billion, or 4.5%, year over year. This includes a bit of a decline in crop cash receipts and a sizable one of 7.6% when it comes to animal and animal product receipts. When it comes to specific crops, we can expect this year to see an increase of 3.4%, though most other categories will see results come in either stable or down.

Management highlighted that while prices for U.S. soybeans and corn were a bit higher than they had been in prior months, they still remained well below what they were from 2021 through about half of 2024. And in Brazil, prices are higher. But because of broader economic weakness there, we are likely to experience some pressure from that market. Management mentioned in their latest earnings call that low commodity prices caused by higher output, on top of high input costs, have proven to be a challenge for the agricultural industry.

And because of those challenges, management is focusing more this year on prices and cost management, on top of operational efficiencies, in order to compete effectively while maximizing shareholder returns. This is not to say that there won't be any opportunity for growth in some respects. Management specified, for instance, that the company landed a project valued at about $80 million in the irrigation space for the MENA region. $70 million of that should be generated this year. This should also help to boost backlog, considering that the project in question came after quarterly results became official. That meant that while backlog did fall from $168.2 million in the first quarter of 2025 to $119.2 million in the first quarter of 2026, the likely effective backlog today is considerably higher.

Keeping in mind that we really don't know what to expect for the rest of the year, I merely projected our performance based on how the first quarter looked. This would give us net profits of $71.1 million, adjusted operating cash flow of $96.4 million, and EBITDA of $102.8 million. Using these estimates, as well as the results that the company achieved for 2025, we can see in the chart above how shares are priced at the moment.

These aren't bad multiples, but they aren't fantastic, either. Typically, I would say that they put the company into the low end of the fair value range. And relative to other similar firms, the stock looks more or less fairly valued also.

In the chart above, you can see that while only one of the five firms that I compared it to ended up being cheaper than it is on a price-to-earnings basis, two of them were cheaper when it came to the other valuation metrics. And specifically, when it came to the price-to-operating-cash-flow approach, a third one was tied with it.

Takeaway

All of the data present right now suggests to me that Lindsay Corporation makes for an interesting prospect. In a more normal economic environment, I might even turn a bit bullish on it. However, because of how the stock is priced, in combination with the fact that we should expect further weakening this year, I think that rating it a ‘hold’ makes a lot more sense. But if management can come out with some really fantastic results and expectations for the fiscal year, I could upgrade it.

data.title: Cabot: Competitive Pressures Due To High Tire Imports Continuing To Weigh
data.description:
Intro

Our last commentary on Cabot Corporation (CBT) was in early January this year, when we maintained our bearish stance on the specialty chemicals and performance materials outfit due to sustained volume pressure in both of its segments, resulting in a guidance cut. Many times, companies gloss over fundamental concerns, especially when they know the fundamentals may paint a different picture. Still, Cabot's management was very candid with its shareholders on the Q4 earnings call, citing sustained trade turbulence as the chief reason why earnings will most likely remain soft for the foreseeable future. The lasting ill effects of automotive production, sluggish PMI trends, and a stagnant construction sector were all called out by management on the Q4 earnings call, so for this, the company must be commended for being loyal to its shareholders.

Although the stock is more or less flat since our bearish rating earlier this year, the release of the company's first quarter earnings report (Released 2/3/2026) once more highlighted the concerns we have with this play. Although Battery Materials revenue was definitely a bright spot in the first quarter ending 12/31/2025, volumes came in down in both the Reinforcement Materials and Performance Chemicals, highlighting sustained macro-led pressure.

Reinforcement Materials' EBIT Drops $22 Million In Q1

As we see below, Cabot's biggest segment is "Reinforcement Materials," where the segment's EBIT dropped by approximately $28 million in the first quarter to come in at $102 million. Although EBIT in "Performance Chemicals" grew by $3 million in Q1 to come in at $48 million, its gains are not enough at this juncture to compensate for the poor production levels in the tire space, considering its margins are on an even keel with "Reinforcement Materials."

Downsizing Operations To Align With New Normal

Although management pointed to cash-flow trends, battery materials, and Indonesian and Mexican assets as areas of strength, investors shouldn't dismiss the heavy cost-cutting initiatives that the company has been undergoing, as well as the reduced capex spend. In essence, this means (at least over the near term) that Cabot doesn't deem it correct to aggressively invest through this current downcycle. Remember, the market (investors) is a predictive mechanism that is always attempting to ascertain where earnings will be over a forward-looking 12- to 18-month period.

This means that if reduced investment and further targeted cost-cutting programs are going to remain in situ, this essentially means Cabot is getting smaller, demonstrating less potential to grow its earnings aggressively. To this point, earnings are expected to fall by approximately 14% in fiscal 2026 (Ending 9/30/2026), although this number will most likely deteriorate further to the downside given the number of downward EPS revisions in recent months.

The CEO on the recent Q4 earnings call pointed to reducing network capacity to align with this ''new normal'' in the carbon-black utilization space

Finally, as a result of the declining carbon black utilization levels in Western geographies, we believe it is prudent to look at our network capacity and align it to current demand levels. With this in mind, we are finalizing plans to rationalize carbon black capacity in the Americas and Europe to position us to operate more efficiently, enhance profitability and maintain flexibility as we navigate this challenging demand environment. We will communicate any decisions when they are made.

Therefore, let's dig into some charts to demonstrate why we are maintaining our bearish stance in Cabot. Being chartists, we believe all of the company's known fundamentals have already been baked into the price, which means there is always a distinct correlation between Cabot's fundamentals (cause) and resulting technical trends (effect). Furthermore, before we study the charts, investors should remember that short-term trends invariably transition into intermediate trends and consequently become long-term trends over time. Therefore, although long-term charts are the "standard" for long-term investors, shorter-term charts are key in that they help us ascertain where the long-term chart is trending, all things remaining equal.

Long-Term 20-Year Chart

As we see below, although the long-term bullish trendline dating back to 2009 remains intact, the breach of the depicted trendline (dating back to 2020) and moving average crossover demonstrates consolidation is the "best-case" scenario over the near term. The risk here for the technical trader or value investor is that technically (from a MACD, RSI, and Stochastics standpoint) and fundamentally (From a GAAP earnings standpoint - P/E of 12.72-Trailing), the stock looks attractive when compared to historic trends. While this may be so, Cabot's growth profile (Especially regarding top-line revenues) has collapsed in recent times, with prolonged negative growth expected in 2026. Suffice it to say, Cabot is cheap for a reason, where we believe all of the bad news has yet to be priced into this play.

Intermediate 5-Year Chart

As we see below, CBT stock had been holding above the $70 area from late 2024 to October of last year. However, the plunge below this support level and beneath $60 a share means Cabot is now playing "catch-up," now having a surmountable amount of resistance to overcome. Furthermore, the declining histogram demonstrates that the rally out of the November 2025 lows has been losing its potency, signaling likely lower lows ahead, which the monthly chart may not yet be pricing in.

Conclusion

To sum up, we are reiterating our Sell rating in Cabot due to concerning technical trends and sustained pricing pressure problems, resulting in bearish EPS trends and a reduced capital spending budget. Let's see what the second quarter holds. We look forward to continued coverage.

data.title: Immersion: No Point In Starting A Position At Such A Messy Time
data.description:
Introduction

It’s been almost two years since I decided to cover Immersion Corporation (IMMR), and the decline of its share price prompted me to look into it once again and give some color on what has happened since then and why such a decline has occurred.

Currently, there are a lot of risks around the restatement delays, which could push the company to be delisted. Besides that, the company’s lumpy revenue business model and how they go about getting that revenue are still unappealing to me. I hope that the current investors or potential investors will weigh their relationship with IMMR and decide what is best for their money.

The Beginning Of Troubles - BNED Accounting Issues

It seems that the issues were identified in 2025. Immersion and Barnes & Noble (BNED) audit committee investigations found some accounting issues, which led to a lot of filings being in need of restatement. The core issues at hand were found to be unsupported manual journal entries that seem to have reduced the cost of sales in the fiscal 2024 and 2025 periods and errors in revenue recognition for the First Day Complete program. The revenue under this program was recognized too early because opt-out assumptions were incorrect and later were reversed when the actual opt-out rates were known. The full explanation can be found in the link here.

Here is an excerpt:

The Investigation is now complete and, based on the Investigation, the Barnes & Noble Education Audit Committee concluded that a former Barnes & Noble Education Payment Processing employee (whom Barnes & Noble Education had terminated) made unsupported manual journal entries that improperly reduced cost of sales in the fiscal years ended April 27, 2024 and May 3, 2025.

And;

The Investigation also concluded that certain revenue recognized in the third quarter of fiscal 2025 related to First Day Complete was incorrectly accelerated based upon incorrect optout assumptions; this revenue automatically reversed in the fourth quarter of fiscal 2025 based on actual opt-out rates.

These issues stem from the BNED side and flow down into IMMR’s consolidated reporting. Unfortunately, the auditors found the issue to be quite serious, which meant a lot of the prior financial statements will have to be restated, going as far back as the June 30th ’24 quarter. So, right now, the backlog is quite large, and the company has been battling with Nasdaq delinquency notices, and as of the latest update from Nasdaq, IMMR received a delisting notice over these delays.

These manual journal entries were linked to an improper reduction in the cost of sales, which affected BNED’s accrued liabilities and accounts receivable. IMMR owns around 30% of BNED, so this is materially affecting IMMR’s financials.

So far, we can see that IMMR managed to get the Q1’26 statement published just today, the 26th of March, for the quarter ending July 31 ’25.

It looks like it wasn’t an easy fix, as many reports had to be restated. This is where the situation stands currently. The company still needs to restate quite a few reports; however, it is getting rather risky right now because of the missed deadline for restatements, which could mean a delisting is in the cards.

IMMR - Core Business Update

Because of the delays in filings, it is hard to see the full picture of IMMR’s operations. These were not really affected by the whole fiasco at BNED, but we still cannot see how well it performed. If we look at the latest published statement, we are two quarters behind. For the three months ended July 31st ’25, royalty income was down 92% from the same time last year. BNED’s revenues more than doubled to $288m, so BNED remains the saving grace for IMMR’s lumpy revenues. Nothing really changed in the core haptics licensing business. It will always be this lumpy, but since the company is changing the way it is reporting BNED in its consolidated financial statements, the core business is going to take the backseat because BNED shows a lot more consistent revenue and growth than its haptics business. BNED overshadows what IMMR is at its core, making it a completely different company, in my opinion. In the restated FY25, IMMR's haptics business made $74m for the full year, while BNED made close to $1.5B. Now, with the haptics business falling back down once again to under $4m, it’s negligible. The main business is not doing the numbers that investors were hoping for. I’m actually not sure if investors even care about the core business at this point, as most of them are focused on the BNED investment that hasn’t done any good so far, as all it has given to the investors was another risk source due to the whole restatement mess and dilution of their shares. Or, in other words, a massive headache. Since my previous article on the company, the share price has halved, and with the current situation, I don’t see any floor just yet.

Let’s look at the company’s lagging financial position. IMMR had around $155m in liquidity and $170m in long-term debt. With the current lows of the haptics performance and very thin BNED margins, the company isn’t able to fund the interest expenses on the debt outstanding just from operations, so it’s a good thing they have a decent amount of cash. Its operating cash flow was also negative at around -$61.6m. It’s not great, but it has improved compared to the same quarter the previous year.

Closing Comments

The management needs to get its ship in order and give a swift update on what is happening and where this company is heading. I feel like it is not going to be the haptics business in the future, or at least it is not going to put so much emphasis on it and will focus on its BNED position. Like a holding company.

I’ve said in my first article that I like the technology behind the company, but the way it operates through litigation and patents is just not an appealing model at all. I don’t see a reason to own it if you are not bag-holding already through this messy time. I am quite curious to see what is going to happen over the next while. Are the investors going to get a relief rally, which will allow them to cash out, or will we see a delisting and further price declines if the financial statement mess doesn’t improve shortly?

data.title: PFFA Vs. FPE: Where Investors Get The Best Blend Of High Yield And Diversification
data.description:
Intro

While it’s always important to balance risk within a portfolio, during uncertain times, it becomes even more important to put more focus on investments that are less correlated with the main indexes. As is often the case, it’s easier said than done — just like many things in life.

As a rule of thumb, the much‑discussed bonds‑versus‑equities split is usually a starting point when one is thinking about portfolio diversification. While there are some exceptions, most of the time the two don’t correlate much. Between them, you can include every other asset class — commodities, REITs, cryptocurrencies, and various alternatives such as option‑based strategies.

If we look at each asset class more closely, they all have their own subdivisions: in commodities, you have precious metals like gold and silver, and in energy, you have oil and gas, and so on. Bonds can be viewed along a spectrum of risk, starting with Treasuries at the lower‑risk end and extending to junk bonds at the higher‑risk end. In equities, the lower‑risk end might be a boring grocery retailer, while the riskiest end could be a small AI play with no real earnings but an impressive story and an elevated stock price.

There’s one asset category that often gets less attention – preferred stocks. Continuing the previous line of thought, I see them sitting somewhere between high‑yield dividend stocks and high‑yield (junk) bonds in terms of risk. They’re technically equities, but in practice, they share more similarities with bonds.

Asset Manager Virtus, managing the Virtus InfraCap US Preferred Stock ETF (PFFA) built on preferred stocks, offers the following definition and illustrative chart:

Preferred stock is a class of capital equity that pays regular dividends at a specified rate and has priority over common stock, but not corporate debt, in the payment of dividends and in any liquidation of the corporation’s assets (hence the term “preferred”).

Preferreds are often described as “income hybrid” securities, since they exhibit characteristics of both stocks and bonds, potentially enabling them to play a powerful diversifying role vis-à-vis traditional balanced stock and bond portfolios.

Virtus also shares its perspective on why investors might choose to put their hard-earned money into preferred securities:

Preferred stocks have tended to be overlooked and underutilized by many investors, despite their many potential benefits:

  • Yield advantage: Historically, preferred stocks’ dividend yield has been competitive with other income-oriented assets, including high-yield bonds – and has usually been higher than the dividend yield on the issuer’s common stock and the coupon payment on its bonds.
  • Payment priority: Preferred stock shareholders have priority claim over common stock shareholders to the corporation’s assets in the event of bankruptcy/liquidation. They also stand ahead of common stockholders for dividend payouts.
  • Limited rate risk: Preferred stocks’ yield advantage may help offset the negative price impact of rising interest rates. The fixed-to-floating coupon structure of many preferreds may also help mitigate rate risk by reducing their sensitivity to rate changes.
  • Tax-favored income: Dividend payments are typically taxed at lower rates than other types of income for corporations and individuals, magnifying preferreds’ after-tax returns.
  • Lower correlations: Historically, preferreds have been only moderately correlated with traditional stocks and bonds, and even less correlated with 10-year Treasuries, providing potential diversification benefits.

To sum up, Preferreds are an attractive asset class that offers both high yield and diversification. They’re clearly better suited for regular income seekers, because — much like bonds — their upside is limited. When equities go on strong bull runs, preferreds usually don’t follow. Their performance depends far more on yield than on the underlying company’s quarterly operational results.

That said, Preferreds are still not fully capped. They are sensitive to interest rates. When rates fall, preferred prices tend to rise, just like bonds, but the move tends to be less pronounced.

In my view, the ideal environment for preferreds is a normal, steady market — not too bullish and not sharply bearish. A moderate economic cooldown often works well for them. But if the economy drops rapidly, preferreds can still sell off more than bonds because of their higher risk profile, even though they’re more protected than common equity.

Why It’s Smarter to Invest in Preferreds Through ETFs

Preferreds come with a whole set of terms that can be highly complex to grasp. They’re not just about yield and duration, or yield to call, as is often said when it comes to preferreds. Each issue can have its own quirks, and many have multiple yield phases — for example, a fixed‑rate period followed by a fixed‑to‑floating period. In that sense, the preferred market can feel a bit like the Wild West, and in my view, it’s better to let seasoned managers with deep experience in this field handle them.

The Best ETF Alternatives

I checked the most actively traded ETFs focused on preferreds using a screener and—perhaps not too surprisingly given our earlier discussion—found that the best‑performing funds are the actively managed ones. This supports my view that preferreds are complex enough that having an experienced manager is genuinely valuable.

Looking at the 3-year chart, PFFA stands out as the clear winner with a total return of 48.83%, followed by FPE at 36.05%. The rest of the funds are all passive ETFs.

I’m comparing the top performers, PFFA and FPE, to weigh each ETF’s strengths and weaknesses and see how they measure up side by side. In my opinion, this kind of head‑to‑head comparison gives a better view of each fund’s quality than evaluating them in isolation.

I’ll start with a brief overview of the funds.

Virtus InfraCap US Preferred Stock ETF (PFFA):

PFFA is a leveraged fund. As highlighted in their prospectus, the fund:

…may purchase and write put and call options in an effort to generate additional income, reduce volatility and/or hedge against market or other risks in the Fund’s portfolio. The Fund will also borrow from banks for investment purposes generally in an amount between 15% and 25% of its net assets, although it may borrow an amount up to 33 1/3% of its total assets..

In an excellent interview worth checking out, the fund manager shares that PFFA hasn’t used options strategies since recovering from the COVID drawdown.

Since PFFA is leveraged, the fund's total expense ratio of 2.11%, highlighted on their webpage, also factors in borrowing costs. The base management fee is 0.80%.

The distribution rate has just climbed above 10%, while the SEC yield stands at 9.14%:

The top 10 holdings are as follows:

...and the breakdown of sector allocations:

The First Trust Preferred Securities and Income ETF (FPE):

Fund Investment Strategy:

The First Trust Preferred Securities and Income ETF is an actively managed exchange-traded fund. The fund's investment objective is to seek total return and to provide current income. Under normal market conditions, the fund invests at least 80% of its net assets (including investment borrowings) in preferred securities and income-producing debt securities including corporate bonds, high yield securities and convertible securities.

In summary, PFFA appears to be better diversified in terms of sector allocation, with financials making up 33.1% of its exposure, whereas FPE has a combined 71% in banks, insurance, and capital markets. Exposure to international markets adds an extra layer of risk reduction for FPE.

All things considered, the funds have relatively similar characteristics, but the key point is that PFFA has shown a better total return and currently offers a much stronger yield. It does carry a higher risk profile since it’s a leveraged fund, but as long as the economy is largely in a normal shape and avoiding major disruptions, I think the risk is well-rewarded.

Takeaway

In my view, PFFA offers the better risk‑reward profile of the two, and I rate it as a Buy. It’s a well‑managed and well‑diversified fund that has also significantly outperformed its peers in recent years. I believe it provides valuable diversification during turbulent periods. FPE is a solid fund as well, but with 71% exposure to financials — especially given the heightened risks in private credit — I think it’s prudent to mitigate that concentration for now, so I rate the fund as Hold.

Investors should remember that preferred stocks can, under certain conditions, behave much like regular equities. During periods of strong market turbulence, they often get sold off along with the broader market. That said, as long as we’re dealing with a normal correction — which is part of healthy market behavior — drawdowns in preferreds also push yields higher, offering income‑focused investors attractive payouts while waiting for markets to recover.

data.title: Netstreit: A Retail REIT That Has Outshined The Texas Sun, And Keeps Growing
data.description:
In today's article, I pick a REIT out of my hat full of REIT stocks, and the lucky one this time is Netstreit Corp. (NTST). This is a retail REIT for which Mizuho raised its price target this month, keeping its outperform view, while Raymond James also raised its price target to $22 despite a slight downgrade on the stock.

Since I first recommended holding on to this stock in my May 2025 article, it is up around +14%, and also up since my subsequent Buy ratings in Sep. 2025 and Jan. 2026, proving that my view about future growth in this stock would materialize.

Key points driving my confidence so far were portfolio expansion in the U.S. Sunbelt, as well as low over-concentration in a single tenant.

My follow-up today will make use of the latest data from its early February earnings results as part of my 8-point holistic rating review.

Thesis Summary

After my follow-up research on Netstreit, I am reaffirming my prior Buy rating. The worksheet below shows how the positive strengths and upside clearly overwhelm a few areas of concern:

Today's article will showcase how, despite some market turbulence and shocks lately, there is reason to keep believing in this type of retail REIT but also to be confident in its portfolio expansion, net-lease business model, impressive margins, proven dividend growth, and investment-grade rating from Fitch.

At the same time, what kept it from being a strong buy was a lower price forecast and technical patterns hurting that type of thesis at this time.

Macro & Sector Outlook

In this section, I gave this stock a buy due to several favorable factors.

When considering the macroeconomic factors that could impact this sector, I considered both the possible trajectory that could impact this REIT's retail tenants and also population/demographic trends and the supply/demand picture for retail REITs.

Consider that a positive tone was struck by industry association National Retail Federation, whose forecast this month said that "retail sales in 2026 will grow by 4.4% over 2025 to $5.6T," adding that "solid underlying fundamentals, particularly income growth, household balance sheets, and labor market stability, are expected to support continued consumer activity in 2026."

I think this sort of tone is just the sort of support that retail REIT investors are looking for right now, in the midst of all the macro volatility lately.

If we are to bake in the recent geopolitical and oil shocks, there are still some positive figures in terms of economic growth in the U.S. For instance, on Thursday, CNBC wrote that the OECE "sees gross domestic product in the U.S. accelerating at a 2% pace this year before easing to 1.7% in 2027."

In terms of regional demographics, despite somewhat of a cooling down in southeast U.S. regional inflows of population, TD data shows it still leads the U.S. in terms of net migration growth, which I think could favor REITs invested heavily in the Sunbelt:

Also, the lower supply lately of new retail construction to meet the demand could favor established, existing REITs like Netstreit. Consider that JLL pointed out last year that cost inflation continued to be a headwind, saying

"rising costs continue to outpace rents; at the same time, strong demand for alternative uses like multifamily and mixed-use increases the opportunity costs for building spaces that are entirely retail."

So, my bullishness is driven by supply/demand, demographics, and continued economic health of the US.

Competitive Positioning

When it comes to competitive positioning, this is a new section I did not include in some of my prior coverages of this stock, and from this additional angle, I also gave the stock a buy score.

For this part, I picked 3 retail REITs to be peer comparables: Whitestone REIT (WSR), FrontView REIT (FVR), and Kimco Realty (KIM).

From peer data, Netstreit is in a respectable top-line growth position, with a 5-year dividend CAGR of +42%, putting it 2nd in this group behind the powerhouse FrontView; however, return on equity was its weak point, with an ROE of just 0.50%.

Where Netstreit also showed some promise was in its price momentum over the last decade vs. the S&P 500 (SP500), and while it did not beat that benchmark, neither did any of its peers, and it actually was 2nd in this peer group, so it shows that over a longer period of various market cycles, investors showed lots of support for this stock.

Looking beyond just those metrics, as a baseline to even compete seriously in this peer group, I think what favors Netstreit is a sizeable portfolio diversified across many U.S. states and tenants, and in fact, according to their last investor deck (pg. 5), they are in 45 states and have well over 100 tenants, with a nearly perfect occupancy level.

Thus, I'm leaning on the bullish side here too on the basis of portfolio competitiveness, price return, and proven revenue growth, despite some weaker ROE numbers.

Company Growth Factors

When it comes to the potential for company growth, I gave the stock a Strong Buy rating, and here is why.

A key growth factor in equity REITs that I bring up is net portfolio growth, and Netstreit fits that profile, as its Q4/end of FY25 earnings release (pg. 2) showed this REIT with net investment growth (more investments than dispositions) during FY25, which I think matters as it can drive future rental income flows.

Of course, pure portfolio expansion just for the sake of expansion is ignoring the importance of portfolio recycling sometimes to gain exposure to property with better ROI, and Netstreit is doing that, with CEO Mark Manheimer commenting in Q4 results that "we accretively recycled capital throughout the year, exceeding our portfolio diversification goals for 2025."

Complementing the above growth trajectory is Seeking Alpha data showing expected fwd revenue growth of nearly +19%, easily beating the sector average, and something I think will attract bullish investors who place a lot of weight on top-line growth potential.

Hence, this scenario, plus the 99.9% occupancy rate that I highlighted earlier, made me highly bullish in this section.

Profitability Metrics

When it comes to continued earnings growth potential, I gave this stock a buy.

For this part, I considered metrics related to a REIT's earnings, including the EBITDA margin and FFO/AFFO growth.

Where Netstreit really impressed was with an EBITDA margin of nearly +79% and a positive upward trend (shown by the dark blue line), the best in this peer group and indicating the core business (ignoring the effects of depreciation) is strong at generating earnings, and I think investors really should focus on that.

Because of various market cycles and shocks in the short term, I next considered the FFO growth over 5 years to smooth things out, and Seeking Alpha data shows a 5-year FFO CAGR of +18.6%, much higher than the sector average, with the AFFO CAGR for that period at +13.6%.

As a "net lease" type of REIT, this model could continue favoring Netstreit to keep property expenses down, which I think can be a major cost driver for equity REITs, particularly in a period of cost inflation.

Consider, for example, from income statement data, that YoY property expenses only grew around 9%, while total revenues grew around +19%, which is quite a nice spread.

To also consider another angle, the analyst consensus forecasts a 5% to 7% YoY FFO growth this year and next, so that type of confidence should complement any bullish view, and that is exactly what I gave this stock in this section.

Balance Sheet Risk

In this next section, I actually was even more confident and gave the stock a strong buy score, and balance sheet risk is an especially important topic to REITs considering the size of their portfolios.

When it comes to balance sheet risk, I considered credit ratings, the D/E ratio, and asset risk exposure too.

Notable to mention is that this REIT is covered by major agency Fitch, which gave it an investment-grade BBB- last year. Interesting to me from those Fitch comments was "stable operating performance as demonstrated by resilient pandemic-era performance, with occupancy over 99%," which I think is a good sign of this REIT's resilience through such a huge market shock, while I've seen some hotel REITs having had to cut their dividends during that time, in fact.

The D/E is another impressive metric for this REIT; at just 0.77, it not only is the lowest in this peer group but also has shown a declining trend if you look at the line, which could indicate less leverage exposure vs. equity.

Of course, some readers may say this set of peers is not an exact 1-to-1 comparison since a net-lease model may have certain advantages over others, but I wanted to do a broader sampling of retail REITs, particularly to include fellow Texas-based REITs like Whitestone and FrontView, but also Kimco with its diversified retail portfolio.

That brings me to the topic of tenant diversification, which is a key risk to consider in equity REITs, given that overexposure to a single tenant or operator could raise the risk needle and expose this REIT's asset portfolio to increased tenant risk.

Consider from the Q4 investor deck (pg. 5), which came out after my last article on this stock, within its top 10 tenant list no single tenant drives more than 5% of ABR/annualized base rent, and notice that there are several major retail brands in their tenant list that could be considered "defensive" niches like grocery/pharmacy (CVS, Walgreens, Walmart, and Ahold Delhaize - owner of chains like Stop & Shop and Food Lion).

Therefore, I am compelled to be highly bullish on this stock in this category, based on a low D/E, low tenant risk exposure, and an investment-grade rating from Fitch.

Dividend Outlook

After considering the dividend outlook, I gave the stock a Buy score in this section.

For this section, I considered yield, growth, and safety/coverage.

With a fwd yield of 4.7% (as of Thursday), it trails some of the high-yield REITs I cover or invest in, which tend to be in the niche of mortgage REITs. One example would be AGNC (AGNC). So, Netstreit's yield seems just above some US Treasury money market funds rather than high-yield REITs, so my view is a neutral one here.

However, it has proven itself as a long-term dividend grower, with a 5-year dividend CAGR of +11.4%, easily beating the sector average.

In terms of dividend coverage, consider that its quarterly dividend is $0.22/share and its most recent reported quarterly AFFO/share was in the $0.32 - $0.34 range, according to income statement data, and this implies a 1.4x - 1.5x dividend coverage, which easily covers the dividend.

So, I am modest about the yield but love the proven growth and coverage, and hence am modestly bullish here.

Valuation Metrics

Using Seeking Alpha data, I made the following valuation and price forecast worksheet, which supports a neutral/hold score in this category:

Although the latest avg. Wall St. target expects a +15% upside; my own target was more muted at just 5% upside, supporting more of a hold than a buy in this case.

The stock is not a major outlier to this peer group in terms of valuation, so it could be the market expects it to perform close to the peer group. I would argue that it is not being generous enough, considering the margins and FFO growth I showed earlier and the positive analyst consensus.

Technical Charting

After considering the following chart, which follows the share price over the last year vs. the 50-day and 200-day SMA, if I were to consider only technical patterns and not fundamentals in the rest of the article, I'd give it a hold.

The stock already had its buy signal last spring when the 50-day crossed above the 200-day (golden cross formation); however, right now the support from the 50-day SMA has been broken. A positive sign is that both moving averages are continuing in an upward direction, so some may argue that this "dip" is simply due to the recent market and oil shock impacting many equities, and it is a time to buy this dip.

I am not yet convinced of that, so I am neutral here, and although this section lowers the overall score somewhat, it did not ultimately hurt my bullish view too much.

Risks and Challenges to Thesis

A key risk to my bullish thesis is tied to what I mentioned earlier, and that is the inflationary cost environment for construction, which, combined with an elevated interest-rate environment on commercial loans, could continue being a headwind to growth through development, and that also includes the high cost of land suitable to build on and acquire.

What could mitigate that in the near term and future is both a reduction in inflation and a drop in the cost of long-term financing, and this is something many equity REITs could benefit from, and not just this one, as it lowers the cost of capital.

Conclusion

To wrap up today's discussion, I reaffirmed my prior buy rating and agreed with the Wall Street consensus (as of Thursday), which was also a buy.

Today's article showed how upside can potentially come from continued portfolio growth while also maintaining low property expense and high margins, as well as keeping leverage risk low.

Netstreit should be added to your watchlist for further research as a long-term play in the retail REIT space and also as a dividend income idea.

data.title: VCX: Do Not Overpay For Anthropic And OpenAI
data.description:
Summary

Fundrise Innovation Fund (NYSE: VCX) is a U.S.-listed Closed-End Fund (CEF) that aims to provide retail investors access to late-stage, venture-backed technology companies. These high-growth and private names include Anthropic (ANTHRO), Databricks, and OpenAI (OPENAI). As of January 29, 2026, VCX self-reported a Net Asset Value (NAV) of $18.19 per share across 28.3 million shares outstanding, implying net portfolio assets of roughly $515M.

VCX opened at $31.00 on its first day of trading, representing a 1.7x premium to NAV.

As of March 26, 2026, the fund trades at $258.99 per share. While VCX’s underlying portfolio is composed of private companies that have shown rapid growth, the implied valuations based on their NAV premium are fundamentally untenable in my opinion. At current prices, VCX’s top three holdings imply a combined valuation greater than the combined market capitalization of Nvidia (NASDAQ: NVDA), Apple (NASDAQ: AAPL), Alphabet (NASDAQ: GOOG), Microsoft (NASDAQ: MSFT), Amazon (NASDAQ: AMZN), and TSMC (NYSE: TSM). While these companies collectively generate hundreds of billions in annual free cash flow, the implied valuation of VCX suggests their core three holdings are greater than all six combined. As a result, I rate VCX a Strong Sell.

Fund Structure

To understand VCX’s current valuation, we must first distinguish between a CEF and a standard Exchange-Traded Fund (ETF). ETFs typically trade at the combined value of their underlying holdings. This is due to a “creation and redemption” arbitrage protocol that provides price stability. When an ETF deviates from its NAV, Authorized Participants (“APs”) intervene to profit from this arbitrage.

Premium: Should an ETF trade above its NAV, APs purchase the underlying portfolio of securities and redeem them for new shares of the ETF and sell those shares to profit.

Discount: Should an ETF trade below its NAV, APs purchase shares of the ETF and exchange the shares for the underlying assets and sell those assets to profit.

This continuous arbitrage loop ensures that an ETF’s market price rarely deviates from its NAV.

As a CEF, VCX operates with a fixed amount of issued shares and does not possess this creation and redemption protocol. Once the shares are issued, these shares are priced solely by the market supply and demand. As a result, traditional CEFs often trade at a discount or premium to their NAV, depending on market sentiment.

In exchange for exposure to private companies, VCX charges an annual management fee of 1.85%. The fund carries no performance fee or carried interest, which is atypical relative to traditional venture capital structures. VCX pays no dividends, tracks no index, and is actively managed by Fundrise Advisors.

Holdings

Looking at VCX’s portfolio, we can see that it is top-heavy, with Anthropic, Databricks, and OpenAI alone representing 48.3% of net assets. Unlike an index-based ETF, VCX relies entirely on Fundrise’s deal flow and internal valuation methodology. A single adverse valuation event across any of their top holdings will have a material impact on reported NAV. More importantly, any valuation adjustment will not be reflected publicly until the end of the quarter.

As of March 26, 2026, VCX trades at $258.99 per share, implying a 14.24x NAV multiple. The table above cross-references the most recent valuations of Anthropic, Databricks, and OpenAI with the implied valuation of each company based on VCX’s market price.

At current levels, VCX implies a combined valuation for Databricks, OpenAI, and Anthropic of $19.42T. To put this into context, six of the largest publicly traded companies in the world, NVDA, AAPL, GOOG, MSFT, AMZN, and TSMC, represent just $18T in combined market cap. These are also companies that have very strong profit margins and generate cash at a high rate.

Anthropic and OpenAI, on the other hand, continue to burn capital at a significant rate, with Databricks only recently having reached positive cash flow. To put it succinctly, the implied valuations cannot be justified based on market fundamentals, in my opinion.

Lessons in History

The best comparable fund to understand VCX is Destiny Tech100, a closed-end fund that listed in 2024. DXYZ also aimed to provide retail investors with public access to private companies. DXYZ’s core holding is SpaceX, with additional positions in Databricks, OpenAI, xAI, and Shield AI. Similar to VCX, DXYZ has a fixed share count, illiquid private companies, and a retail-driven investor base.

At its peak, DXYZ traded at $70.13 per share, representing approximately 14x its reported NAV. The premium was driven by similar dynamics to VCX’s premium today: constrained float and retail fervor. This premium was unsustainable and collapsed, with shares today having declined 57.5% from its peak.

An important thing to note is that DXYZ’s underlying portfolio has appreciated tremendously during this drop in share price. NAV has increased nearly 4x, rising from $5.07 to $19.97 as of the most recent quarter.

This appreciation has been mostly driven by SpaceX’s increase in valuation and mark-to-market gains across other positions. Despite this increase in NAV, the premium has compressed from 14x NAV to approximately 1.5x NAV. An investor who correctly identified DXYZ’s strong portfolio and bought at peak valuation would have suffered a 57.5% drawdown despite the underlying portfolio growth. Today, VCX sits at 14.24x NAV, similar to DXYZ’s peak premium.

Key Risks

Lockup Expiry

VCX is thinly traded due to a lockup that prevents investors prior to 02/20/26 from selling their shares until September 2026. Daily volume has been very low, with only hundreds of thousands of shares trading hands. I believe the supply constraint has been pivotal for this premium increase. When the lockup lifts, many investors will face an enormous incentive to sell to crystallize their unrealized profits. The resulting wave of share sales could be violent and rapid, causing a compression in NAV premium and a resulting drop in share price.

NAV Compression

The single greatest risk to owning VCX at current levels is NAV compression. VCX is trading at 14.24x NAV today, roughly equal to DXYZ’s peak premium. Should the market re-rate VCX to a typically generous 1.25x NAV, the implied share price falls to roughly $22.73, representing a 92% decline from current levels.

Private Market Opacity

While the financials of VCX’s underlying private holdings are not publicly disclosed, the generative AI companies that make up the bulk of the portfolio have acknowledged that profitability remains a distant goal. OpenAI alone projects cumulative spending of $600B through 2030, and despite reaching an estimated annualized run rate of $25B, the company’s most recent $120B funding round implies strong future growth to justify their current valuation. Anthropic and Databricks carry similar dynamics, and if there is a down round or negative sentiment, it can ripple throughout the private markets, resulting in a decrease in NAV. Any deterioration in the fundamentals of VCX’s holdings will not be reflected until the end of the quarter, given that these holdings are illiquid.

Macroeconomic Headwinds

The broader macroeconomic environment has been challenging. Ongoing U.S. and Iran tensions have resulted in increasing volatility in the market and, more importantly, resulted in sustained elevated oil prices. As a result, inflation has run hotter than the Federal Reserve’s goal, and rate cut expectations have decreased significantly.

Looking at December 2026, we can see the market is pricing in a 52.2% probability of zero rate cuts, a 36.5% probability of one rate hike, and a 9.4% probability of two rate hikes. This environment is bearish for broad market equities and especially bearish for illiquid and unprofitable growth companies, which is the entire portfolio of VCX.

Conclusion

VCX is a genuinely well-thought-out fund. Fundrise has created a well-constructed vehicle that gives retail investors access to a portfolio that was previously reserved for institutional investors. Rather than having to wait for an IPO that may have taken years, retail investors can now freely gain access to popular companies such as Anthropic, OpenAI, and Databricks. Their growth as companies has been rapid and attracted billions of dollars in investment. However, at its current share price of $258.99 per share, VCX’s premium to NAV is untenable in my opinion. DXYZ has already shown us what happens when CEFs trade at a substantial premium to their NAV and retail momentum fades. Until their share price corrects to be more in line with their NAV, I must assign VCX a Strong Sell.

data.title: Mach Natural Resources: NG Pivot Meets AI Power Surge With Double-Digit Yield
data.description:
Introduction

Back when I first covered Mach Natural Resources LP (MNR), I highlighted their strong fundamentals, robust cash generation capacity, and great margin of safety thanks to their attractive valuation, with their massive $1.3 billion acquisitions nearly doubling their production and boosting their exposure to long-life, low-risk natural gas assets across the U.S.

Following a solid report and a pivotal 2025, MNR remains a Buy, with the valuation still implying a significant discount compared to their real long-term potential, although we should be aware of the volatility risk (including downward pressure), especially because of the Iran conflict.

Natural Gas Pivot Advances

Mach reported a solid year that helped transform the business further, with the CAD reaching $274.39 million, which may seem like a stable increase, but it represents about $2.086 per unit, meaning a significant drop from the $2.723 per unit seen in 2024 given the high amount of shares they issued to fund their massive $1.3 billion worth of acquisitions (~40% debt and ~60% stock).

This represents a significant drop of about 23.4% YoY, but it’s clearly explained by the drop in oil prices (offset by the jump in gas) and higher costs, while reserves more than doubled YoY, reaching a PV-10 of $3.1 billion.

For 2026, they plan to invest a very solid $315 million to $360 million compared to $251.9 million in 2025, with net production expected to reach between 150 MBoe/d and 157 MBoe/d, identical to the previous expectations but now assuming a higher differential compared to Henry Hub, noting that the Anadarko and San Juan basis widened compared to HH while drilling will pivot towards natural gas. The company’s CEO mentioned that this differential widening doesn’t seem like a long-term concern, however.

And then I think the takeaway isn't an issue. So if you look back over 5 years in the San Juan, the production is the same. So it's not driven by oversupply to increase or loosen the basis. And the same way in the Anadarko. We're not seeing this from a supply perspective. So it's just a weather in for a fairly warm winter that has widened basis, in my opinion.

Financially, based on MNR’s latest report, we can see a solid position overall, with the current assets covering their current liabilities and a significant jump in partners’ capital despite the rise in debt for the $1.3 billion deal, although we should keep in mind that it comes as a result of the 60% equity funding.

Mach distributed a strong $244.5 million in 2025, meaning the equivalent of a 10.36% yield at the moment of writing this. Meanwhile, the company also announced a very solid $0.53 distribution, nearly doubling it from the $0.27 seen in Q4’25, slightly better than the $0.50 seen in Q1’25, but well below the levels from 2024, which goes to show the kind of volatility we should keep in mind when it comes to this company.

Iran Boost Should Not Last

Right now, the main factor behind the company’s jump has been the ongoing situation in Iran, which caused massive jumps in global oil, gas, and many other prices. Although we see some mixed signs towards a recovery following the 15-point plan proposed by the U.S. and Iran reportedly considering fees for passage through the Strait of Hormuz, it’s important to understand that this is not necessarily a good thing for a company like MNR except for this near-term boost.

Oil and gas prices are not in a great spot, as they would theoretically drop regardless since the weak fundamentals of the oil and gas industry seen even before the conflict would then take over. If anything, any bit of extra time at such prices can be even more detrimental for the market, potentially leading to a higher-for-longer rate environment and continued fear of inflation that can push the Fed into even more pauses, as we should keep in mind that the role of rate hikes was to fight inflation in the first place.

However, I mentioned during the Tourmaline Oil (TRMLF) stock analysis - Canada’s largest natural gas producer that I highly recommend you check out - that I don’t expect this to last for that long, which should be good, but that’s still an unpredictable factor to keep in mind, and if it drags on for long enough, a recession is more than likely.

The situation in Venezuela is also advancing, with Exxon Mobil (XOM) - the same company that called it “uninvestable” not long ago - announcing that their team is now in the country, evaluating the prospects of a potential re-entry. Still, given the high amount of investments and politico-economic uncertainty, it’s very likely that this will not have any significant effects on the market, at least in the foreseeable future.

Regardless, when it comes to a company like MNR, I mentioned during the previous coverage how this remains a long-term play first and foremost, especially after the massive pivot into natural gas, with Goldman Sachs expecting massive potential for power demand coming from data centers, while the National Center for Energy Analytics recently highlighted that the current surge in AI's power demand should require an increase of 10% to 20% in overall U.S. gas production by 2030.

Risks Reiterated

Despite this long-term potential, however, investors should always keep in mind that this is inherently a volatile business, as it depends on commodity prices that are dictated by politics, wars, speculation, fear, etc.

Meanwhile, their distributions will also depend on this, and even though I still see a solid average yield on this in the long term, we should not expect a linear yield from commodity companies, while potential cuts during bad times can cause even more selling pressure.

Valuation

Starting from the company's CAD of $274.39 million in 2025 (realized prices of $66 for oil, $3 for gas and $23 for liquids, which seems like a conservative long-term estimate), I'll assume a moderate 3.5% CAGR over the first years given their higher investments, potential accretion, cost savings, and improvements, followed by a 3% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and 2.5% terminal growth rate this time, with a moderate 12.5% discount rate to take into account the volatility and risks of the industry and still demanding a strong margin of safety, with 168.21 million units outstanding as of the end of Q4.

This way, we get to an equity value of $2.97 billion, for an intrinsic value of $17.64 per unit, which is still above the current $14.30 despite the much higher spot prices.

In other words, this company still appears to be very undervalued regardless of the current Iran conflict, although we should keep in mind that the near term will very likely be volatile and can result in a fall, but the long term remains strong.

Conclusions

MNR remains a Buy, as the valuation continues to offer a significant disconnect compared to their potential regardless of the near-term boost coming from the Iran conflict.

There are risks to keep in mind, and we should consider volatility a natural part of this, but I see it as an opportunity to add a strong yield at a discount instead of a negative, looking forward to taking advantage of lower prices in the future.
